teachingPoint: Service code never imports a provider SDK directly. All infrastructure access (database, queue, AI, RAG, secrets, flow engine) goes through abstract fabric interfaces resolved at runtime via dependency injection. This makes every provider swappable without changing service code.
positiveExample:
// @Inject(DATABASE_SERVICE) private db: IDatabaseService
// await this.db.storeDocument('products', data, id);

teachingPoint: If a business user might want to change a value — it must live in a FREEDOM configuration document in Elasticsearch, not in compiled TypeScript code. This is the FREEDOM Machine boundary: business-configurable values go to FREEDOM docs; fixed algorithmic logic stays in MACHINE code. MACHINE code cannot be overridden by tenant config.

All service methods return DataProcessResult<T>. Never throw for business logic conditions. Use DataProcessResult.failure('CODE', 'message') for expected failures. Use DataProcessResult.success(data) for success. Only let unexpected exceptions propagate as-is — catch and wrap them in a try/catch returning DataProcessResult.failure.

storeDocument() must be called BEFORE enqueue(). The database record must exist before downstream consumers receive the event. If the process crashes after storeDocument() but before enqueue(), the record exists and the event can be re-emitted. If the process crashes after enqueue() but before storeDocument(), the consumer fires on a record that does not exist — data loss.

Tenant scope is automatic via AsyncLocalStorage. Fabric providers read TenantContext internally. Never pass tenantId as a parameter to fabric methods. The caller cannot forget to scope — the fabric enforces it. When a service needs to know the current tenantId for business logic (e.g., storing it in a document), read it from the TenantContext, not from a method parameter.

No entity-specific controllers, services, or classes. One DynamicController handles all CRUD for all entity types via /api/dynamic/{indexName}. Business data uses Record<string, unknown> — not TypeScript classes. Schema lives in config documents (FREEDOM), not TypeScript type definitions. This allows the engine to support unlimited entity types without code changes.

All inter-service events use the CloudEvents envelope format via createCloudEvent(). Never fetch() or make HTTP calls to another service — all communication is async via queue events. This decouples services, enables retry/DLQ, and makes the system auditable. The CloudEvents envelope includes source, type, specversion, id, time, and data fields.
positiveExample: // await this.queue.enqueue('order.created', createCloudEvent({ source: 'orders', type: 'com.xiigen.order.created', data: orderData }));
negativeExample:
// WRONG: const res = await fetch('http://inventory-service/api/reserve', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(data) });
// Direct HTTP between services bypasses queue, breaks retry, and creates coupling
